topics.meaning: Anything available in our environment that can be used to satisfy our needs, provided it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable, is called a resource.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Everything in our environment that can satisfy human needs is a resource, but only if it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable. Resources are not free gifts of nature; they are a function of human activities, because human beings transform material available in the environment into usable resources using technology. Human beings themselves are essential components of resources, and they create institutions to accelerate economic development. The process of transformation of things involves an interactive relationship between nature, technology and institutions. For example, coal lying underground becomes a resource only when humans develop the technology to mine and use it. Thus resources depend as much on human ability as on natural availability.
topics.easyExplanation: A resource is anything from nature that humans can use to meet their needs, once they have the technology, money and acceptance to use it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the material
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Locate something in the environment such as water, soil, coal or sunlight that could be useful.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check technological access
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: See whether technology exists to reach and use that material, like machines to mine coal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check economic feasibility
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Confirm that using the material is affordable and profitable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check cultural acceptability
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ensure society is willing to use the material.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Transform into a resource
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Humans apply technology and institutions to turn the material into a usable resource.

examples: Petroleum buried deep underground becomes a resource only after technology to drill and refine it is developed.
Sunlight becomes a resource when solar panels (technology) make it economically usable for electricity.

importantKeywords.definition: Anything in the environment usable to satisfy human needs when technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Iron ore becomes a resource when used to make steel.

importantKeywords.definition: The application of knowledge and tools to use natural materials.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Drilling machines used to extract oil.

importantKeywords.definition: Organised structures and rules created by humans to accelerate development.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A government department that plans mining.

topics.quickSummary: A resource is anything in the environment that can satisfy human needs, provided it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable. Resources are not free gifts of nature but a function of human activity; people transform raw material into resources through an interactive relationship between nature, technology and institutions. Humans are themselves an essential component of resources.

Nature, technology and institutions are interdependent.

topics.meaning: Resources are classified on the basis of origin, exhaustibility, ownership and status of development.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Resources can be classified in four main ways. On the basis of origin they are biotic (obtained from the biosphere with life, like humans, flora and fauna) or abiotic (composed of non-living things, like rocks and metals). On the basis of exhaustibility they are renewable (which can be renewed or reproduced, like solar energy, water and forests) or non-renewable (which take millions of years to form and get exhausted, like minerals and fossil fuels). On the basis of ownership they are individual, community, national or international resources. On the basis of status of development they are potential, developed stock or reserves. This classification helps in proper planning and management of resources.
topics.easyExplanation: Resources are sorted into groups based on where they come from, whether they can run out, who owns them, and how developed they are.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By origin
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide resources into biotic (living, like fish) and abiotic (non-living, like rocks).

stepByStepExplanation.title: By exhaustibility
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Separate renewable (water, solar) from non-renewable (coal, minerals).

stepByStepExplanation.title: By ownership
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Classify as individual, community, national or international.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By development status
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Group as potential, developed stock or reserves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply for planning
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the classification to plan judicious use and conservation.

examples: Solar energy and forests are renewable resources, while coal and petroleum are non-renewable resources.
A public park is a community resource, while minerals within 12 nautical miles of the coast are national resources.

importantKeywords.definition: Resource obtained from the biosphere that has life.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Fish, forests and livestock.

importantKeywords.definition: Resource that can be renewed or reproduced over a short time.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Solar energy and wind.

importantKeywords.definition: Materials in the environment that we cannot use due to lack of technology.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Hydrogen and oxygen in water as a source of energy.

topics.quickSummary: Resources are classified four ways: by origin (biotic or abiotic), by exhaustibility (renewable or non-renewable), by ownership (individual, community, national, international) and by status of development (potential, developed stock, reserves). revisionNotes: Origin: biotic vs abiotic.

topics.topic: Development of Resources and Sustainable Development
topics.meaning: Indiscriminate use of resources has caused serious problems, so sustainable development that does not harm the environment or future generations is essential.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Resources are vital for human survival and quality of life, but the wrong belief that they are free gifts of nature led to their indiscriminate use. This caused three major problems: depletion of resources for the greed of a few, accumulation of resources in few hands dividing society into haves and have-nots, and global ecological crises such as global warming, ozone layer depletion, environmental pollution and land degradation. To avoid this, sustainable development is needed, which means development should take place without damaging the environment and present development should not compromise the needs of future generations. Gandhiji warned that there is enough for everybody's need but not for anybody's greed. Such concern led to global efforts for resource conservation.
topics.easyExplanation: Using resources carelessly harms the planet, so we must develop in a way that protects the environment and keeps enough for future generations.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognise the problem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Understand that careless use depletes resources and harms the environment.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the crises
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note global warming, ozone depletion, pollution and land degradation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define sustainable development
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Development without damaging the environment or future needs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Adopt the principle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Follow Gandhiji's idea: enough for need, not for greed.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Act globally
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Support international efforts for resource conservation and equity.

examples: Global warming and ozone layer depletion are ecological crises caused by indiscriminate use of resources.
Using solar power instead of coal is a step toward sustainable development.

importantKeywords.definition: Development without damaging the environment and without compromising future generations' needs.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Replanting forests after cutting trees.

importantKeywords.definition: Using up resources faster than they can be replenished.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Overusing groundwater till wells dry up.

importantKeywords.definition: Decline in the quality of land due to misuse.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Soil losing fertility from overuse of chemicals.

topics.quickSummary: Indiscriminate use of resources, caused by the belief that they are free, led to depletion, unequal accumulation and ecological crises like global warming and pollution. Sustainable development, meaning growth without harming the environment or future generations, is essential. Gandhiji's warning that there is enough for need but not for greed captures this idea.
revisionNotes: Three problems: depletion, unequal accumulation, ecological crises.

Gandhiji: enough for need, not for greed.

topics.topic: Rio de Janeiro Earth Summit and Agenda 21
topics.meaning: The 1992 Rio de Janeiro Earth Summit and Agenda 21 were major international efforts for environmental protection and sustainable development.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In June 1992, more than 100 heads of states met at the first International Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, to address urgent problems of environmental protection and socio-economic development at the global level. The leaders signed the Declaration on Global Climatic Change and Biological Diversity. The Rio Convention endorsed the global Forest Principles and adopted Agenda 21 for achieving sustainable development in the 21st century. Agenda 21 is the declaration signed by world leaders in 1992 at the United Nations Conference on Environment and Development. It aims at global sustainable development and seeks to combat environmental damage, poverty and disease through global cooperation based on common interests, mutual needs and shared responsibilities. One major objective is that every local government should draw its own local Agenda 21.
topics.easyExplanation: In 1992 world leaders met at Rio de Janeiro and agreed on Agenda 21, a plan to protect the environment and develop the world sustainably.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Convene the Summit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Over 100 heads of states met at Rio de Janeiro in June 1992.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sign declarations

stepByStepExplanation.title: Endorse Forest Principles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Rio Convention endorsed the global Forest Principles.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adopted Agenda 21 to achieve sustainable development in the 21st century.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Asked every local government to draw its own local Agenda 21.

topics.meaning: Resource planning is a widely accepted strategy for the judicious and balanced use of resources, especially important for a diverse country like India.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Planning is the widely accepted strategy for the judicious use of resources, and it is very important in a country like India that has enormous diversity in resource availability. Some regions are rich in certain resources but deficient in others; for example, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh are rich in minerals and coal, while Arunachal Pradesh has abundant water resources but lacks infrastructure, and Rajasthan is rich in solar and wind energy but lacks water. Resource planning in India involves three stages: identification and inventory of resources across regions through surveying and mapping; evolving a planning structure with appropriate technology, skill and institutions for implementation; and matching resource development plans with overall national development plans. Mere availability of resources is not enough; technology, quality of human resources and institutions are also needed for development.
topics.easyExplanation: Resource planning means using resources wisely and in a balanced way, which India needs because resources are spread unevenly across its regions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify resources
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Survey, map and measure the resources in each region.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Build planning structure
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Set up technology, skills and institutions to use the resources.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Match with national plans
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Align resource development with overall national development goals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Address regional imbalance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Balance resource-rich and resource-poor regions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add human and institutional inputs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine resources with skilled people and institutions for real development.

examples: Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh are rich in minerals and coal but lag in development, showing the need for planning.
Rajasthan has plenty of solar and wind energy but lacks water, so planning must balance resources.

importantKeywords.definition: Strategy for judicious and balanced use of resources.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mapping coal reserves before mining them.

importantKeywords.definition: Identification, surveying and mapping of available resources.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Listing all the forests in a state.

importantKeywords.definition: Uneven distribution of resources across regions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Some states rich in coal, others rich in water.

topics.quickSummary: Resource planning is the strategy for judicious use of resources, vital in diverse India where regions differ widely in resource availability. It involves three stages: identification and inventory of resources, evolving a planning structure with technology and institutions, and matching resource plans with national development. topics.meaning: Land is a vital natural resource used for forests, cultivation, pastures, settlements and other purposes, and India has varied relief features supporting these uses.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Land is a natural resource of utmost importance; we live on it, perform economic activities on it and use it in many ways. India has land under a variety of relief features: about 43 per cent of the land area is plain, providing facilities for agriculture and industry; mountains account for about 30 per cent and ensure perennial flow of rivers and tourism; and about 27 per cent is plateau, which has rich reserves of minerals, fossil fuels and forests. Land resources are used for several purposes, including forests, land not available for cultivation (barren land and land put to non-agricultural uses), other uncultivated land excluding fallow land (pastures, tree crops and culturable waste land), fallow lands and net sown area. The land use pattern depends on physical factors like topography, climate and soil, and human factors like population density and technology.
topics.easyExplanation: Land is a precious resource used for forests, farming, grazing, buildings and more, and India's plains, mountains and plateaus support these different uses.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note relief features
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: India has plains (43%), mountains (30%) and plateaus (27%).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use for forests
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Land under forest cover supports ecology and wildlife.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate non-cultivable land
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Identify barren land and land used for buildings, roads and factories.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mark uncultivated land
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Include pastures, tree crops and culturable waste land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Measure net sown area
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Calculate land actually sown with crops in a year.

examples: Plains, which form about 43 per cent of India's land, provide facilities for agriculture and industry.
Land put to non-agricultural uses includes buildings, roads and factories.

importantKeywords.definition: The area sown with crops in a year.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A wheat field actively cultivated this season.

importantKeywords.definition: Land left uncultivated for more than five agricultural years.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: An abandoned field overgrown with weeds.

Land is used for forests, non-agricultural purposes, pastures, fallow lands and net sown area. The land use pattern depends on physical factors like relief, climate and soil and human factors like population and technology.

topics.meaning: Land degradation is the decline in land quality caused by human activities, and it can be controlled through conservation measures.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Human activities such as deforestation, overgrazing, mining and the over-irrigation of land have contributed greatly to land degradation. Mining sites are abandoned after the work is over, leaving deep scars and overburdening of debris; in states like Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh and Odisha deforestation due to mining has caused severe land degradation. In Gujarat, Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh overgrazing is a main reason for land degradation, while in Punjab, Haryana and western Uttar Pradesh over-irrigation causes waterlogging leading to an increase in salinity and alkalinity of the soil. Mineral processing like grinding of limestone for cement and calcite for ceramics generates dust that retards infiltration of water. Land degradation can be reduced through afforestation, proper management of grazing, planting shelter belts, control of mining activities, and proper discharge and disposal of industrial wastes.
topics.easyExplanation: Land degradation means land losing its quality due to deforestation, overgrazing, mining and over-irrigation, and it can be reduced by planting trees and managing land carefully.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify causes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note deforestation, overgrazing, mining and over-irrigation as causes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe effects
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: See scars from mining, waterlogging, salinity and dust pollution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plant trees
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use afforestation and shelter belts to bind the soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Manage grazing and mining
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Control overgrazing and regulate mining activities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dispose wastes properly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Discharge and dispose industrial waste after treatment.

examples: Overgrazing causes land degradation in Gujarat, Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh.
Over-irrigation in Punjab and Haryana causes waterlogging and increased soil salinity.

importantKeywords.definition: Decline in the quality and productivity of land.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mined land left as barren scars.

importantKeywords.definition: Excessive grazing by animals that strips vegetation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Too many cattle eating grass on a pasture.

importantKeywords.definition: Rows of trees planted to reduce wind erosion.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Trees along desert fields to stop sand movement.

topics.quickSummary: Land degradation, the decline in land quality, is caused by deforestation, overgrazing, mining and over-irrigation, leading to scars, waterlogging, salinity and dust pollution in various states. It can be controlled through afforestation, regulated grazing, shelter belts, control of mining and proper disposal of industrial wastes.
revisionNotes: Causes: deforestation, overgrazing, mining, over-irrigation.
Over-irrigation causes waterlogging, salinity and alkalinity.
Mining leaves scars and debris.
Conservation: afforestation, shelter belts, waste management.

topics.meaning: India has a variety of soils including alluvial, black, red and yellow, laterite, arid and forest soils, each with distinct features and crops.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Soil is the most important renewable natural resource, formed over millions of years by weathering of rocks, temperature changes and the actions of running water, wind and decomposers. India has several major soil types. Alluvial soil is the most widely spread and fertile soil, found in the northern plains and deposited by rivers like the Ganga; it is rich in potash, phosphoric acid and lime and ideal for crops like paddy, wheat and sugarcane. Black soil, also called regur soil, is ideal for growing cotton and is found in the Deccan trap region of Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat. Red and yellow soils develop on crystalline igneous rocks in low rainfall areas of the Deccan plateau. Laterite soil forms under tropical climate with alternate wet and dry seasons and supports tea, coffee and cashew. Arid soils are sandy and saline, found in western Rajasthan, while forest soils occur in hilly and mountainous areas.
topics.easyExplanation: India has many soils like fertile alluvial soil for wheat, black soil for cotton, red, laterite, arid and forest soils, each suited to particular crops.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Alluvial soil
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Most fertile, found in northern plains, good for wheat, paddy and sugarcane.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Black soil
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Regur soil ideal for cotton, found in the Deccan trap region.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Red and yellow soils
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Develop on igneous rocks in low rainfall Deccan areas.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Laterite soil
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forms with alternate wet and dry seasons, good for tea, coffee and cashew.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Arid and forest soils
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Arid soils sandy and saline in Rajasthan; forest soils in hilly areas.

examples: Black soil (regur) of Maharashtra and Gujarat is ideal for growing cotton.
Alluvial soil of the northern plains is highly fertile and suitable for wheat and sugarcane.

importantKeywords.definition: Fertile soil deposited by rivers in the plains.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Soil of the Ganga plain growing wheat.

importantKeywords.definition: Clayey soil that holds moisture, ideal for cotton.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Deccan trap soil used for cotton farming.

importantKeywords.definition: Soil formed by intense leaching under wet and dry climate.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Soil of Western Ghats growing tea and coffee.

topics.quickSummary: Soil is the most important renewable resource. India has alluvial soil (most fertile, northern plains, wheat and sugarcane), black or regur soil (Deccan, cotton), red and yellow soils (igneous rocks, low rainfall), laterite soil (wet and dry climate, tea and coffee), arid soils (sandy, saline, Rajasthan) and forest soils (hilly areas). Each soil suits particular crops.

Black (regur) = cotton, Deccan trap.
Laterite = tea, coffee, cashew.
Arid soils sandy and saline in Rajasthan.

topics.meaning: Soil erosion is the removal of the soil cover faster than it can form, and it is prevented through conservation methods like contour ploughing and terracing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The denudation of the soil cover and its subsequent washing down is called soil erosion. Normally the processes of soil formation and erosion go on simultaneously and there is a balance between the two, but this balance is sometimes disturbed by human activities like deforestation, overgrazing and faulty farming, as well as by natural forces like wind and water. Running water cuts through clayey soils and makes deep channels called gullies, creating badland topography like the ravines of the Chambal basin. When water flows as a sheet over large areas down a slope it washes away the topsoil, called sheet erosion. Wind blows loose soil off flat or sloping land in wind erosion. Soil conservation methods include contour ploughing along contour lines, terrace farming on steep slopes, strip cropping in large fields, and planting shelter belts of trees to check wind erosion.
topics.easyExplanation: Soil erosion is the washing or blowing away of fertile topsoil, and it can be prevented by ploughing along contours, making terraces and planting trees.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand erosion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Soil erosion is the removal of soil cover faster than it forms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify types
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Gully erosion forms ravines, sheet erosion removes topsoil, wind erosion blows soil away.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Contour ploughing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plough along contour lines to slow water flow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Terrace and strip cropping
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cut terraces on slopes and grow strips of grass between crops.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plant shelter belts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plant rows of trees to break wind and check erosion.

examples: Gully erosion has created badland or ravines in the Chambal basin.
Terrace farming on the steep slopes of the western and central Himalayas reduces soil erosion.

importantKeywords.definition: Removal of the soil cover and its washing or blowing away.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Topsoil washed off a sloping field by rain.

importantKeywords.definition: Ploughing along contour lines to slow water and check erosion.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Furrows made across a slope instead of down it.

importantKeywords.definition: Cutting steps on hill slopes to grow crops and reduce erosion.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Step-like fields on Himalayan hillsides.

topics.quickSummary: Soil erosion is the denudation and washing away of the soil cover faster than it forms, caused by deforestation, overgrazing and natural forces. Types include gully erosion (ravines), sheet erosion and wind erosion. It is controlled by contour ploughing, terrace farming, strip cropping and shelter belts of trees.
revisionNotes: Soil erosion = removal of soil cover.
Types: gully (ravines), sheet, wind.
Chambal basin has badland/ravines.
Conservation: contour ploughing, terracing, strip cropping, shelter belts.

topics.meaning: Biodiversity is the immense variety of living organisms, and India is one of the world's richest countries in terms of its flora and fauna.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We share the planet with millions of other living beings, from micro-organisms and bacteria to banyan trees, elephants and blue whales, all forming a complex web of ecological system in which humans are only a part and very much dependent on the system for survival. Plants, animals and micro-organisms re-create the quality of the air we breathe, the water we drink and the soil that produces our food. Biodiversity, or biological diversity, is immensely rich in wildlife and cultivated species, diverse in form and function but closely integrated through multiple networks of interdependencies. India is one of the world's richest countries in terms of its vast array of biological diversity, possibly twice or thrice the number yet to be discovered. Forests play a key role in the ecological system as they are the primary producers on which all other living beings depend.
topics.easyExplanation: Biodiversity is the huge variety of plants, animals and tiny organisms, and India has one of the richest collections of living things in the world.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define biodiversity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It is the immense variety of all living organisms on Earth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See interdependence
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: All living beings form a web depending on one another.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand ecological role
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants and microbes clean air, water and soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note forests' role
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forests are primary producers supporting all life.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognise India's richness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: India is among the world's richest in flora and fauna.

examples: India's biodiversity ranges from micro-organisms and bacteria to banyan trees, elephants and blue whales.
Forests act as primary producers on which all other living beings depend.

importantKeywords.definition: The variety of all living organisms and their interrelationships.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Many species of plants and animals in a forest.

importantKeywords.definition: The plant life of a particular region.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Trees and shrubs of the Western Ghats.

importantKeywords.definition: The animal life of a particular region.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Tigers and elephants of Indian forests.

topics.quickSummary: Biodiversity is the immense variety of living organisms, closely integrated through interdependencies. Humans are only one part of this ecological web and depend on it for clean air, water and soil. Forests are key primary producers. India is one of the world's richest countries in biological diversity, with much still to be discovered.

topics.meaning: Plants and animals are classified by the IUCN into categories such as normal, endangered, vulnerable, rare, endemic and extinct based on their survival status.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) classifies species into several categories based on their status of survival. Normal species are those whose population levels are considered normal for survival, such as cattle, sal and pine. Endangered species are in danger of extinction if the negative factors continue, like the black buck, crocodile, Indian wild ass and Indian rhino. Vulnerable species are those whose population has declined to a level from which they are likely to move into the endangered category, such as the blue sheep, Asiatic elephant and Gangetic dolphin. Rare species have small populations and may become endangered or vulnerable, like the Himalayan brown bear, wild Asiatic buffalo and desert fox. Endemic species are found only in particular areas, like the Andaman teal and Nicobar pigeon. Extinct species have already disappeared, such as the Asiatic cheetah and pink-headed duck.
topics.easyExplanation: Species are sorted into groups like normal, endangered, vulnerable, rare, endemic and extinct depending on how safe their survival is.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Population at safe levels, like cattle and pine.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Endangered species
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In danger of extinction, like the Indian rhino and black buck.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Vulnerable species

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rare species

stepByStepExplanation.title: Endemic and extinct
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Endemic species are local-only; extinct ones have disappeared, like the Asiatic cheetah.

examples: The Indian rhino and black buck are endangered species in India.
The Asiatic cheetah and pink-headed duck are examples of extinct species.

importantKeywords.definition: Species in danger of extinction if threats continue.

importantKeywords.definition: Species found only in a particular area.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The Nicobar pigeon of the Nicobar Islands.

topics.topic: Causes of Depletion of Forest and Wildlife
topics.meaning: Forests and wildlife are depleting due to deforestation for agriculture, mining, development projects, overgrazing and forest fires.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The greatest damage to Indian forests and wildlife has come from human activities. The expansion of railways, agriculture, commercial and scientific forestry and mining activities since colonial times caused large-scale depletion. Between 1951 and 1980, over 26,200 sq km of forest area was converted into agricultural land all over India. Large-scale development projects have also contributed; since 1951 over 5,000 sq km of forest was cleared for river valley projects, and clearing continues even today for projects like the Narmada Sagar Project in Madhya Pradesh. Mining is another important factor, as in the Buxa Tiger Reserve in West Bengal, which is seriously threatened by dolomite mining. Grazing and fuel-wood collection also degrade forests, and the unequal access, control and sharing of resources by different social groups deepen the problem. The destruction of forests and wildlife is linked to the loss of cultural diversity too.
topics.easyExplanation: Forests and wildlife are shrinking because land is cleared for farming, mining, dams and roads, along with overgrazing and forest fires.

topics.meaning: Conservation preserves the ecological diversity and life support systems and protects the genetic diversity of plants and animals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Conservation in the background of rapid decline in wildlife population and forestry has become essential. Conservation preserves the ecological diversity and our life support systems such as water, air and soil, and it also preserves the genetic diversity of plants and animals for better growth of species and breeding. For example, in agriculture we are still dependent on traditional crop varieties, and fisheries depend heavily on the maintenance of aquatic biodiversity. In the 1960s and 1970s conservationists demanded a national wildlife protection programme, and the Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act was implemented in 1972 with provisions for protecting habitats. An all-India list of protected species was published, hunting was banned, legal protection was given to habitats and trade in wildlife was restricted. Central and state governments set up national parks and wildlife sanctuaries, and several projects were announced to protect specific gravely threatened animals.
topics.easyExplanation: Conservation protects forests, wildlife and the natural systems we depend on, and keeps a variety of plants and animals alive for the future.

topics.meaning: Project Tiger is a major wildlife conservation programme launched in 1973 to save the dwindling tiger population in India.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Tiger is one of the key wildlife species in the faunal web. In 1973 the authorities realised that the tiger population had dwindled to 1,827 from an estimated 55,000 at the turn of the century. The major threats to tiger population include poaching for trade, shrinking habitat, depletion of prey base species, growing human population and the trade of tiger skins and use of their bones in traditional medicines, especially in Asian countries, which left the tiger on the verge of extinction. Since India and Nepal provide habitat to about two-thirds of the surviving tiger population in the world, these two nations became prime targets for poaching and illegal trading. Project Tiger, one of the well-publicised wildlife campaigns in the world, was launched in 1973. Tiger conservation is viewed not only as saving an endangered species but as preserving biotypes of sizeable magnitude. Tiger reserves include Corbett in Uttarakhand, Sunderbans in West Bengal, Bandhavgarh in Madhya Pradesh and Sariska in Rajasthan.

topics.meaning: India's forests are classified by ownership and management into reserved, protected and unclassed forests.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Much of India's forest and wildlife resources are either owned or managed by the government through the Forest Department or other government departments, and they are classified into three categories. Reserved forests are the most valuable as far as the conservation of forest and wildlife resources is concerned; more than half of the total forest land has been declared reserved forests, and they are regarded as permanent forest resources for the production of timber and other forest produce and for protective reasons. Protected forests are guarded against any further depletion; almost one-third of the total forest area is protected. Unclassed forests are other forests and wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals and communities. Reserved and protected forests together are also referred to as permanent forest estate. Madhya Pradesh has the largest area under permanent forests, while Jammu and Kashmir, Andhra Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Kerala, Tamil Nadu and other states have a bulk of forest under reserved category.

topics.topic: Community and Government in Conservation (JFM and Sacred Groves)
topics.meaning: Local communities and the government work together to conserve forests through programmes like Joint Forest Management and traditions like sacred groves.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Conservation projects in India are now beginning to focus on biodiversity rather than on just a few of its components. Local communities are increasingly involved in the management and restoration of forests. In Sariska Tiger Reserve, villagers have fought against mining by citing the Wildlife Protection Act, and in Alwar district of Rajasthan the inhabitants of five villages declared 1,200 hectares of forest as the Bhairodev Dakav Sonchuri to protect the wildlife against outside encroachments. The famous Chipko movement in the Himalayas successfully resisted deforestation and showed that community afforestation with indigenous species can be enormously successful. Nature worship is an age-old tribal belief that all creation of nature is sacred; in many cultures, patches of forest called Sacred Groves (the forests of God and Goddesses) are left untouched in their pristine form. The Joint Forest Management (JFM) programme, started in 1988 in Odisha, involves local communities in protecting and managing degraded forests in return for benefits like a share of forest products.

topics.topic: Project Elephant and Other Conservation Projects
topics.meaning: Besides Project Tiger, India runs conservation projects like Project Elephant and gives legal protection to many threatened animals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The central government has announced several projects for protecting specific animals that were gravely threatened. These include the tiger, the one-horned rhinoceros, the Kashmir stag or hangul, three types of crocodiles (fresh water crocodile, saltwater crocodile and the Gharial), the Asiatic lion and others. More recently, the Indian elephant, black buck (chinkara), the great Indian bustard (godawan) and the snow leopard have been given full or partial legal protection against hunting and trade throughout India. Project Elephant was launched to protect elephants and their habitats and corridors, addressing threats from habitat loss and human-elephant conflict. These projects, along with the network of national parks like Kaziranga (famous for the one-horned rhino) and wildlife sanctuaries, aim to protect specific endangered species and the wider biodiversity that supports them. The protection of crocodiles included a successful crocodile breeding programme.

topics.meaning: Water scarcity is the shortage of water relative to its demand, caused by over-exploitation, excessive use and unequal access among people.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although three-fourths of the earth's surface is covered with water and water is a renewable resource, scarcity of water is a reality in many regions. Water scarcity may be an outcome of large and growing population, greater demands for water and unequal access to it. A large population means more demand for water and more water to produce food, so to meet this demand more dams and wells are dug to draw groundwater, which may lead to falling groundwater levels. Most farmers have their own wells and tube-wells for irrigation, but this over-exploitation reduces water availability for others. Water scarcity may also be caused by an abundance of water but bad quality, as water gets polluted by domestic and industrial wastes, chemicals and fertilizers used in agriculture. Thus water scarcity may exist even in areas that receive plenty of rainfall, and it threatens food security and the livelihood of millions.
topics.easyExplanation: Water scarcity means there is not enough good-quality water for everyone, caused by too many people, overuse, and pollution.

topics.meaning: Multi-purpose river projects are dams that serve several purposes at once, such as irrigation, electricity generation, flood control and water supply.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Dams were traditionally built to impound rivers and rainwater for irrigation, but in recent decades they have become multi-purpose projects, integrating conservation of water with flood control. A multi-purpose project serves several purposes at the same time, such as irrigation, generation of electricity (hydel power), water supply for domestic and industrial uses, flood control, recreation, inland navigation and fish breeding. Jawaharlal Nehru proudly proclaimed dams as the temples of modern India because they would integrate development of agriculture and the village economy with rapid industrialisation and growth of the urban economy. Examples include the Bhakra Nangal Project on the Satluj-Beas river, which is used for hydel power and irrigation, and the Hirakud Project in the Mahanadi basin, which integrates conservation of water with flood control. These projects were intended to be symbols of progress and prosperity for a newly independent country.

topics.meaning: Multi-purpose projects and large dams have caused environmental, social and economic problems, leading to movements against them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although dams were built to control floods, regulating and damming rivers affects their natural flow, causing poor sediment flow and excessive sedimentation at the reservoir bottom. This results in rockier stream beds and poorer habitats for the rivers' aquatic life, and it makes it difficult for aquatic fauna to migrate and spawn. The reservoirs submerge existing vegetation and soil, leading to its decomposition over time. Multi-purpose projects also induce earthquakes, cause water-borne diseases and pests, and lead to pollution from excessive use of water. Big dams have been the cause of many social movements like the Narmada Bachao Andolan and the Tehri Dam Andolan, as they displace large numbers of peasants and tribals without adequate rehabilitation. Ironically, the dams built to control floods have triggered floods due to sedimentation in the reservoir, and big dams have mostly been unsuccessful in controlling floods at the time of excessive rainfall. Inter-state water disputes, like the Krishna-Godavari dispute, also arise over the sharing of river water.
topics.easyExplanation: Big dams cause problems like submerging forests, displacing people, harming aquatic life and triggering disputes, which has led to protests against them.

topics.meaning: Rainwater harvesting is the method of collecting and storing rainwater for future use, a viable alternative to large projects.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Given the disadvantages and rising resistance against multi-purpose projects, the water harvesting system was viewed as a viable alternative, both socio-economically and environmentally. In ancient India there was an extraordinary tradition of water harvesting systems based on in-depth knowledge of rainfall and soil types. People built diversion channels like the guls or kuls of the Western Himalayas for agriculture, practised rooftop rainwater harvesting to store drinking water in Rajasthan, developed inundation channels in the flood plains of Bengal, and converted agricultural fields into rain-fed storage structures like the khadins in Jaisalmer and johads in other parts of Rajasthan. In the semi-arid regions of Rajasthan, particularly in Bikaner, Phalodi and Barmer, almost all houses had underground tanks called tankas connected to sloping roofs to store rainwater, known as palar pani, considered the purest form of natural water. Today rooftop rainwater harvesting is being successfully adapted in places like Gendathur in Mysuru, Karnataka.

topics.meaning: Different regions of India developed traditional rainwater harvesting structures suited to their local conditions, like rooftop systems and bamboo drip irrigation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: People across India developed wide-ranging techniques to harvest rainwater, groundwater, river water and flood water in keeping with local ecological conditions. In hill and mountainous regions, people built diversion channels like the guls or kuls of the Western Himalayas for agriculture. Rooftop rainwater harvesting was commonly practised to store drinking water, particularly in Rajasthan. In the flood plains of Bengal, people developed inundation channels to irrigate their fields. Rooftop rainwater harvesting is the most common practice in Shillong, Meghalaya, even though nearby Cherrapunji and Mawsynram receive the highest rainfall in the world; nearly every household in Shillong has a rooftop harvesting structure that meets 15-25 per cent of the household's water need. In Meghalaya, a 200-year-old system of tapping stream and spring water using bamboo pipes, called bamboo drip irrigation, is prevalent, where about 18-20 litres of water enters the bamboo pipe system and is transported over hundreds of metres. Tamil Nadu is the first state to make rooftop rainwater harvesting compulsory for all houses.

topics.meaning: Ancient India had sophisticated hydraulic structures for storing and managing water, showing a long tradition of water engineering.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ancient India had an extraordinary tradition of water management with sophisticated hydraulic structures. In the first century BC, Sringaverapura near Allahabad had sophisticated water harvesting systems channelling the flood water of the river Ganga. During the time of Chandragupta Maurya, dams, lakes and irrigation systems were extensively built. Evidence of sophisticated irrigation works has also been found in Kalinga in Odisha, Nagarjunakonda in Andhra Pradesh, Bennur in Karnataka, Kolhapur in Maharashtra and other places. In the eleventh century, Bhopal Lake, one of the largest artificial lakes of its time, was built. In the fourteenth century, the tank in Hauz Khas in Delhi was constructed by Iltutmish for supplying water to the Siri Fort area. These examples show that people in ancient and medieval India had advanced knowledge of building hydraulic structures to store and manage water for irrigation and drinking.

topics.meaning: Irrigation is the artificial supply of water to crops, essential in India because of uneven and seasonal rainfall.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Water is a basic input for agriculture, and irrigation provides the artificial supply of water to land for growing crops. Irrigation is essential in India because rainfall is highly seasonal and unevenly distributed, with most rain falling in the monsoon months and large parts of the country remaining dry for the rest of the year. Multi-purpose river projects and dams play a major role in providing irrigation by storing water and supplying it through canals; for example, the canal command areas of projects like the Sardar Sarovar provide assured water supply to drought-prone regions, making them drought-proof. Irrigation increases agricultural productivity, allows the cultivation of more than one crop a year, and supports water-intensive crops like rice and sugarcane in regions that would otherwise be unsuitable. However, over-irrigation can also cause problems like waterlogging and increased salinity of the soil, so water must be used judiciously.

topics.meaning: Conserving and managing water resources is essential to ensure water security, prevent scarcity and protect aquatic ecosystems.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Conservation and management of water resources is essential to safeguard against health hazards, ensure food security, continue livelihoods and productive activities, and prevent degradation of natural ecosystems. Over-exploitation and mismanagement of water resources can impoverish this resource and cause ecological crisis with profound impacts on our lives. Water can be conserved by reviving and adopting traditional rainwater harvesting methods, building rooftop harvesting structures, recharging groundwater through hand pumps and abandoned dug-wells, and recycling and reusing water in industries and homes. Treating industrial effluents and sewage before releasing them prevents pollution of rivers and groundwater. Public awareness, judicious use in agriculture through methods like drip irrigation, and legal provisions such as making rooftop rainwater harvesting compulsory, as in Tamil Nadu, all help conserve water. Since water is a renewable but limited resource, careful management is vital for sustainable existence.

topics.meaning: Primitive subsistence farming is slash and burn agriculture practised on small patches of land with primitive tools and family labour.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Primitive subsistence farming is still practised in a few pockets of India on small patches of land with the help of primitive tools like the hoe, dao and digging sticks, and family or community labour. This type of farming depends upon the monsoon, natural fertility of the soil and suitability of other environmental conditions to the crops grown. It is a slash and burn agriculture, where farmers clear a patch of land and produce cereals and other food crops to sustain their family. When the soil fertility decreases, the farmers shift and clear a fresh patch of land for cultivation, allowing nature to replenish the fertility of the soil that has been used. This type of farming is known by different names in different parts of the world, such as Milpa in Mexico, Conuco in Venezuela, Roca in Brazil and Ladang in Indonesia. In India it is called Jhumming in the North-eastern region, Bewar or Dahiya in Madhya Pradesh, Podu or Penda in Andhra Pradesh and Kumari in the Western Ghats.

topics.meaning: Intensive subsistence farming is labour-intensive farming practised in areas of high population pressure on land, using high doses of inputs and irrigation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Intensive subsistence farming is practised in areas of high population pressure on land. It is a labour-intensive type of farming where high doses of biochemical inputs and irrigation are used to obtain higher production from a limited area of land. Because of the right of inheritance leading to the division of land among successive generations, land-holding sizes have become uneconomically small. Yet farmers continue to take maximum output from the limited land in the absence of an alternative source of livelihood, so there is enormous pressure on agricultural land. This farming is common in the densely populated regions of India, particularly the northern plains and the coastal regions, where rice and other food crops are grown intensively. Farmers use fertilizers, manures and assured irrigation to grow more than one crop a year from the same field.

topics.meaning: Commercial farming uses modern inputs to grow crops for sale, and plantation is a type where a single crop is grown on a large area for industry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The main characteristic of commercial farming is the use of higher doses of modern inputs such as high yielding variety (HYV) seeds, chemical fertilisers, insecticides and pesticides to obtain higher productivity for sale in the market. The degree of commercialisation varies from region to region; for example, rice is a commercial crop in Haryana and Punjab but a subsistence crop in Odisha. Plantation is also a type of commercial farming in which a single crop is grown on a large area; it has an interface of agriculture and industry. Plantations cover large tracts of land, using capital-intensive inputs and migrant labour, and all the produce is used as raw material in respective industries. In India, tea, coffee, rubber, sugarcane and banana are important plantation crops; tea is grown in Assam and North Bengal, and coffee in Karnataka. Since the production is mainly for the market, a well-developed network of transport and communication is essential for plantations.

topics.topic: Cropping Seasons - Rabi, Kharif and Zaid
topics.meaning: India has three cropping seasons - rabi, kharif and zaid - based on the timing of sowing and harvesting.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: India has three cropping seasons: rabi, kharif and zaid. Rabi crops are sown in winter from October to December and harvested in summer from April to June; important rabi crops include wheat, barley, peas, gram and mustard, grown mainly in the north and north-western states like Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, Uttarakhand and Uttar Pradesh, with the western temperate cyclones and the Green Revolution helping their success. Kharif crops are grown with the onset of monsoon and harvested in September-October; important kharif crops include paddy, maize, jowar, bajra, tur (arhar), moong, urad, cotton, jute, groundnut and soyabean, grown in regions like Assam, West Bengal, coastal Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Tamil Nadu and Kerala. Zaid is a short season between rabi and kharif in the summer months, during which watermelon, muskmelon, cucumber, vegetables and fodder crops are grown.
topics.easyExplanation: India grows crops in three seasons: rabi in winter (like wheat), kharif in the monsoon (like rice), and zaid in summer (like watermelon).

topics.topic: Food Crops - Rice, Wheat and Millets
topics.meaning: Rice, wheat and millets are the major food grains of India, each requiring particular climatic and soil conditions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Rice is the staple food crop of a majority of people in India, and the country is the second largest producer of rice in the world after China. It is a kharif crop that requires high temperature above 25 degrees Celsius and high humidity with annual rainfall above 100 cm, growing with irrigation in areas of less rainfall, in regions like Assam, West Bengal, coastal Odisha, Andhra Pradesh and others. Wheat is the second most important cereal and the main food crop in the north and north-western parts of the country; it is a rabi crop requiring a cool growing season and bright sunshine at the time of ripening, with two important wheat zones being the Ganga-Satluj plains and the black soil region of the Deccan. Millets like jowar, bajra and ragi are nutri-cereals; jowar is rain-fed and grown in Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh, while ragi grows well in dry regions on red and sandy soils and is rich in iron, calcium and roughage.

topics.topic: Cash Crops - Sugarcane, Oilseeds, Tea and Coffee
topics.meaning: Sugarcane, oilseeds, tea and coffee are important cash crops grown in India for sale and industry.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: India grows several important cash crops. Sugarcane is a tropical and subtropical crop that grows well in hot and humid climate with temperatures of 21 to 27 degrees Celsius and annual rainfall between 75 and 100 cm; India is the second largest producer after Brazil, and it is the main source of sugar, gur (jaggery), khandsari and molasses, grown mainly in Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu and Bihar. Oilseeds cover about 12 per cent of the total cropped area; main oilseeds are groundnut, mustard, coconut, sesamum, soyabean, castor seeds and sunflower, with groundnut being a kharif crop and Gujarat the largest producer. Tea is an example of plantation agriculture, a beverage crop introduced by the British, grown in Assam, the hills of Darjeeling, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu and Kerala; it needs warm, moist, frost-free climate and is labour-intensive. Coffee, mainly the Arabica variety initially brought from Yemen, is grown in the Nilgiri hills of Karnataka, Kerala and Tamil Nadu.

topics.meaning: Cotton and jute are the major fibre crops of India, used to make cloth, gunny bags and other products.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: India is one of the major fibre-producing countries, with cotton, jute, hemp and natural silk being the four major natural fibres. Cotton is one of the main raw materials for the cotton textile industry; India is believed to be the original home of the cotton plant, and it is the second largest producer of cotton after China. Cotton grows well in the drier parts of the black cotton soil of the Deccan plateau, requiring high temperature, light rainfall or irrigation, 210 frost-free days and bright sunshine; it is a kharif crop that takes six to eight months to mature, grown mainly in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Tamil Nadu, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan and Uttar Pradesh. Jute is known as the golden fibre and grows well on well-drained fertile soils in the flood plains where soils are renewed every year; it is grown mainly in West Bengal, Bihar, Assam, Odisha and Meghalaya, and is used to make gunny bags, mats, ropes, yarn, carpets and other artefacts.

topics.meaning: The government brought institutional and technological reforms, including the Green Revolution and White Revolution, to improve Indian agriculture.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Indian agriculture has long suffered from low productivity, and the government has taken several steps to improve it. Institutional reforms include collectivisation, consolidation of holdings, cooperation and the abolition of zamindari, all of which were given priority to bring institutional changes after independence. The Green Revolution, based on the use of high yielding variety seeds, chemical fertilizers and irrigation, and the White Revolution (Operation Flood) for milk production were important strategies to improve agriculture. The government also provides minimum support price, remunerative and procurement prices for important crops to check exploitation of farmers by middlemen and speculators. Provisions for crop insurance against drought, flood, cyclone, fire and disease, Grameen banks, cooperative societies and banks providing loans at lower interest, the Kisan Credit Card and Personal Accident Insurance Scheme, and special weather bulletins and agricultural programmes on radio and television have also been introduced for the benefit of farmers.

topics.meaning: A mineral is a naturally occurring homogeneous substance with a definable internal structure, classified into metallic, non-metallic and energy minerals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Geologists define a mineral as a homogeneous, naturally occurring substance with a definable internal structure. Minerals are found in varied forms in nature, ranging from the hardest diamond to the softest talc, and a particular mineral is formed from a certain combination of elements depending on the physical and chemical conditions, which gives it a wide range of colours, hardness, crystal forms, lustre and density. For general and commercial purposes, minerals are classified into three groups. Metallic minerals contain metals and are subdivided into ferrous (containing iron, like iron ore, manganese, nickel and cobalt) and non-ferrous (like copper, bauxite, lead, zinc and gold). Non-metallic minerals do not contain metals and include mica, limestone, gypsum and salt. Energy minerals (mineral fuels) include coal, petroleum and natural gas. The mineral content of an ore must be in sufficient concentration to make its extraction commercially viable.

topics.meaning: Minerals occur in different forms such as veins and lodes in igneous rocks, beds in sedimentary rocks, and placer and alluvial deposits.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Minerals are usually found in ores, and they occur in several ways. In igneous and metamorphic rocks, minerals may occur in cracks, crevices, faults or joints; the smaller occurrences are called veins and the larger are called lodes, formed when minerals in liquid, molten or gaseous form are forced upward through cavities and cool as they rise. Major metallic minerals like tin, copper, zinc and lead are obtained from veins and lodes. In sedimentary rocks, minerals occur in beds or layers formed by deposition and concentration in horizontal strata; coal, some iron ore, gypsum, potash salt and sodium salt occur this way. Another mode involves the decomposition of surface rocks leaving a residual mass of weathered material, as in the case of bauxite. Certain minerals occur as alluvial or placer deposits in valley floors and the base of hills, such as gold, silver, tin and platinum. Ocean waters contain minerals like common salt, magnesium and bromine, and the ocean beds are rich in manganese nodules.
topics.easyExplanation: Minerals occur in different ways: as veins and lodes in hard rocks, as layers in sedimentary rocks, as residual deposits like bauxite, and as placer deposits in sands.

topics.meaning: Iron ore is the basic mineral and the backbone of industrial development, with India endowed with abundant good-quality reserves.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ferrous minerals account for about three-fourths of the total value of the production of metallic minerals and provide a strong base for the development of metallurgical industries. Iron ore is the basic mineral and the backbone of industrial development, and India is endowed with fairly abundant and good-quality iron ore. Magnetite is the finest iron ore with a very high content of iron up to 70 per cent and excellent magnetic qualities valuable in the electrical industry. Hematite is the most important industrial iron ore in terms of the quantity used, but has a slightly lower iron content of 50 to 60 per cent. In 2018-19 almost the entire production of iron ore (97 per cent) came from Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Karnataka and Jharkhand. The major iron ore belts in India include the Odisha-Jharkhand belt, the Durg-Bastar-Chandrapur belt, the Ballari-Chitradurga-Chikkamagaluru-Tumakuru belt in Karnataka and the Maharashtra-Goa belt.

topics.meaning: Non-ferrous minerals like copper and bauxite and non-metallic minerals like mica and limestone are important for industries.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-ferrous minerals include copper and bauxite, which play a vital role in metallurgical, engineering and electrical industries. Copper is mainly used in electrical cables, electronics and the chemical industries because it is malleable, ductile and a good conductor; the Balaghat mines in Madhya Pradesh, the Khetri mines in Rajasthan and the Singhbhum district of Jharkhand are leading producers. Bauxite is a clay-like substance from which alumina and later aluminium is obtained; aluminium is important because it combines the strength of iron with lightness, good conductivity and great malleability, and India's bauxite deposits are found mainly in the Amarkantak plateau, Maikal hills and the plateau region of Bilaspur-Katni, with the Koraput district of Odisha being an important deposit. Among non-metallic minerals, mica is made up of plates that split into thin sheets and is indispensable in the electric and electronic industries due to its insulating properties; it is found in the Chota Nagpur plateau, Ajmer in Rajasthan and the Nellore belt of Andhra Pradesh. Limestone is the basic raw material for the cement industry and is essential for smelting iron ore in the blast furnace.

topics.meaning: Minerals are finite and non-renewable, so they must be conserved through planned use, recycling and use of substitutes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We strongly depend on minerals for industry and agriculture, but the total volume of workable mineral deposits is an insignificant fraction, about one per cent of the earth's crust, and we are rapidly consuming these resources. Minerals require millions of years to be created and concentrated, and the geological processes of mineral formation are so slow that the rates of replenishment are infinitely small compared to the present rates of consumption. Mineral resources are therefore finite and non-renewable, and rich mineral deposits are our country's extremely valuable but short-lived possessions. Continued extraction of ores leads to increasing costs as extraction comes from greater depths along with a decrease in quality. A concerted effort must be made to use mineral resources in a planned and sustainable manner. Improved technologies need to be evolved to allow the use of low-grade ores at low costs, and recycling of metals, using scrap metals and other substitutes are important steps in conserving mineral resources for the future.

topics.topic: Conventional Energy - Coal, Petroleum and Natural Gas
topics.meaning: Conventional energy sources include coal, petroleum and natural gas, which are the main commercial fuels of India.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Energy resources are classified as conventional and non-conventional. Conventional sources include firewood, cattle dung cake, coal, petroleum, natural gas and electricity. Coal is the most abundantly available fossil fuel in India and provides a substantial part of the nation's energy needs, used for power generation, industry and domestic needs. Coal occurs in different forms depending on the degree of compression: peat has low carbon, lignite is a low-grade brown coal (with reserves at Neyveli in Tamil Nadu), bituminous coal is the most popular in commercial use, and anthracite is the highest quality hard coal. In India coal occurs in Gondwana deposits (over 200 million years old) in the Damodar valley and tertiary deposits (about 55 million years old). Petroleum is the next major energy source and provides fuel and raw material for petrochemical industries; the chief oilfields are in Mumbai High, Gujarat and Assam, with Digboi being the oldest. Natural gas is an important clean energy resource found with petroleum, with large reserves in the Krishna-Godavari basin.

topics.meaning: Non-conventional energy sources like solar, wind, tidal, geothermal, biogas and atomic energy are renewable and environment-friendly alternatives.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-conventional sources of energy include solar, wind, tidal, geothermal, biogas and atomic energy, which are increasingly important because conventional sources like fossil fuels are limited, polluting and getting exhausted. Nuclear or atomic energy is obtained by altering the structure of atoms, releasing energy as heat, which is used to generate electric power; uranium and thorium are used, and Rajasthan and the Aravalli ranges of Jharkhand have large deposits of uranium, while thorium is found in the monazite sands of Kerala. Solar energy is produced using photovoltaic technology that converts sunlight directly into electricity, and India is becoming a leader in solar energy. Wind power has great potential, with the largest wind farm cluster in Tamil Nadu from Nagercoil to Madurai. Tidal energy can be harnessed in places like the Gulf of Khambhat and Gulf of Kachchh. Geothermal energy uses heat from the earth's interior, as at Parvati valley in Himachal Pradesh, while biogas plants use shrubs, farm waste and animal and human waste to produce gas and improved manure.

topics.meaning: Energy resources must be conserved by promoting renewable sources, using public transport, and avoiding wasteful use of energy.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Energy is a basic requirement for economic development, and every sector of the national economy needs inputs of energy. However, conventional energy resources like coal, petroleum and natural gas are limited and non-renewable, and their excessive use causes pollution and harms the environment. There is an urgent need to develop a sustainable path of energy development, promoting energy conservation and increasing the use of renewable energy sources. As a responsible citizen, energy can be conserved by using public transport instead of individual vehicles, switching off electricity when not in use, using power-saving devices, using non-conventional sources of energy, and checking the power equipment regularly to avoid leakage of electricity. The promotion of energy efficiency, the use of solar and wind power, and a shift toward cleaner fuels are essential for conserving energy resources and protecting the environment for future generations.

topics.meaning: Manufacturing is the production of goods in large quantities by processing raw materials into finished products, and it is vital for economic development.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Manufacturing is the production of goods in large quantities after processing from raw materials into more valuable products. People employed in secondary activities manufacture primary materials into finished goods; for example, paper is manufactured from wood, sugar from sugarcane, iron and steel from iron ore, and aluminium from bauxite. The economic strength of a country is measured by the development of manufacturing industries, because they help modernise agriculture, reduce dependence on agricultural income by providing jobs in secondary and tertiary sectors, bring down regional disparities by establishing industries in tribal and backward areas, and earn precious foreign exchange through exports. Countries that transform their raw materials into a wide variety of finished goods of higher value are prosperous, so India's prosperity lies in increasing and diversifying its manufacturing industries as quickly as possible. Agriculture and industry are not exclusive of each other but move hand in hand, as agro-industries boost agricultural productivity.

topics.meaning: Industries are classified on the basis of raw material, main role, capital investment, ownership and the bulk and weight of materials.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Industries are classified in several ways. On the basis of source of raw materials, they are agro-based (cotton, woollen, jute, silk textiles, rubber, sugar, tea, coffee) or mineral-based (iron and steel, cement, aluminium, petrochemicals). On the basis of their main role, they are basic or key industries that supply raw materials to other industries (iron and steel, copper smelting) or consumer industries that produce goods for direct consumption (sugar, toothpaste, paper, fans). On the basis of capital investment, an industry is small scale if the invested capital is up to a fixed limit, otherwise it is large scale. On the basis of ownership, they are public sector (owned by government agencies like BHEL and SAIL), private sector (owned by individuals or groups like TISCO and Bajaj Auto), joint sector (jointly run by state and individuals like Oil India Limited) or cooperative sector (owned by producers or workers, like the sugar industry in Maharashtra). On the basis of bulk and weight of raw material and finished goods, they are heavy industries (iron and steel) or light industries (electrical goods).

topics.meaning: The cotton textile industry is an agro-based industry that has played a major role in India's economy and is concentrated in Maharashtra and Gujarat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The textile industry occupies a unique position in the Indian economy because it contributes significantly to industrial production, employment and foreign exchange earnings, and it is the only industry self-reliant and complete in the value chain from raw material to the highest value-added products. In ancient India cotton textiles were produced with hand spinning and handloom weaving techniques, and after the eighteenth century power looms came into use. The first successful cotton textile mill was established in Mumbai in 1854. In the early years the cotton textile industry was concentrated in the cotton-growing belt of Maharashtra and Gujarat, with Mumbai, Ahmedabad, Sholapur, Nagpur, Indore, Surat and Vadodara being important centres, because of the availability of raw cotton, market, transport, labour, moist climate and port facilities. Today the industry has spread to many states. Cotton textiles have close links with agriculture and provide a livelihood to farmers, cotton boll pluckers and workers engaged in ginning, spinning, weaving, dyeing, designing and packaging.

topics.meaning: The iron and steel industry is the basic industry on which all other industries depend, concentrated in the Chota Nagpur plateau region.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The iron and steel industry is the basic industry since all other industries, whether heavy, medium or light, depend on it for their machinery. Steel is needed to manufacture a variety of engineering goods, construction material, defence, medical, telephonic, scientific equipment and a variety of consumer goods. Production and consumption of steel is often regarded as an index of a country's development. It is a heavy industry because all the raw materials as well as finished goods are heavy and bulky, entailing heavy transportation costs. Iron ore, coking coal and limestone are required in the ratio of approximately 4:2:1, and some quantity of manganese is also required to harden the steel. The Chota Nagpur plateau region has the maximum concentration of iron and steel industries because of the low cost of iron ore, high-grade raw materials in proximity, cheap labour and vast growth potential in the home market. In 2019 India was the second largest producer of crude steel in the world, and important steel plants include Tata Iron and Steel Company (TISCO), Bhilai, Durgapur, Bokaro, Rourkela and Vijayanagar.

topics.meaning: Aluminium smelting and chemical industries are important manufacturing industries based on minerals and chemicals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Aluminium smelting is the second most important metallurgical industry in India. Aluminium is light, resistant to corrosion, a good conductor of heat, malleable and becomes strong when mixed with other metals, and it is used to manufacture aircraft, utensils and wires, gaining popularity as a substitute for steel, copper, zinc and lead. Aluminium smelting plants are located in Odisha, West Bengal, Kerala, Uttar Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu; bauxite is the bulky raw material used, and a regular supply of electricity and an assured source of raw material at minimum cost are the prime factors for locating the industry. The chemical industry in India is fast growing and diversifying, comprising both large and small scale units, with rapid growth in inorganic and organic sectors. Inorganic chemicals include sulphuric acid, nitric acid, alkalies, soda ash and caustic soda, used to make fertilizers, fibres, plastics, glass, soaps and paper. Organic chemicals include petrochemicals used for making synthetic fibres, rubber, plastics, dyes, drugs and pharmaceuticals, and their plants are located near oil refineries.

topics.topic: Other Industries - Cement, Automobile and IT
topics.meaning: Cement, automobile and information technology industries are important manufacturing industries serving construction, transport and communication.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Several other industries are important to India. The fertilizer industry produces nitrogenous fertilizers (mainly urea), phosphatic fertilizers and complex fertilizers, with Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab and Kerala contributing about half the production. The cement industry is essential for construction such as houses, factories, bridges, roads, airports and dams, and it requires bulky and heavy raw materials like limestone, silica, alumina and gypsum; the first cement plant was set up in Chennai in 1904, and the industry is concentrated in Gujarat due to suitable access to markets in the Gulf countries. The automobile industry manufactures trucks, buses, cars, motorcycles, scooters and three-wheelers, and after liberalisation new models stimulated demand; it is located around Delhi, Gurugram, Mumbai, Pune, Chennai, Kolkata, Lucknow and Bengaluru. The information technology and electronics industry covers products from transistors to telephones, computers and telecom equipment, and Bengaluru has emerged as the electronic capital of India, with other centres at Mumbai, Delhi, Hyderabad, Pune and Chennai.

topics.meaning: Industries pollute the environment by causing air, water, land, thermal and noise pollution, degrading the quality of life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Industries have contributed significantly to economic development and progress, but at the same time they have led to environmental degradation. Industries are responsible for four types of pollution: air, water, land and noise. Air pollution is caused by the presence of high proportion of undesirable gases such as sulphur dioxide and carbon monoxide, along with airborne particulate materials like dust, sprays, mist and smoke, and toxic gas leaks can be hazardous, as seen in the Bhopal gas tragedy. Water pollution is caused by organic and inorganic industrial wastes and effluents discharged into rivers, with paper, pulp, chemical, textile, dyeing, tanneries and other industries being major polluters. Thermal pollution of water occurs when hot water from factories and thermal plants is drained into rivers and ponds before cooling. Wastes from nuclear power plants and weapons production cause cancers and birth defects. Noise pollution results from industrial and construction activities, machinery and equipment, causing irritation, hearing impairment and stress.

topics.meaning: Industrial pollution can be controlled by minimising water use, treating wastes, harvesting rainwater and reducing emissions and noise.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every litre of waste water discharged by industry pollutes eight times the quantity of fresh water, so controlling industrial pollution is essential to prevent the degradation of land, water and air. Water pollution can be controlled by minimising the use of water for processing by reusing and recycling it in two or more successive stages, harvesting rainwater to meet water requirements, and treating hot water and effluents before releasing them into rivers and ponds. Effluents should be treated in three phases: primary treatment by mechanical means through screening, grinding, flocculation and sedimentation; secondary treatment by biological processes; and tertiary treatment by biological, chemical and physical processes including recycling of wastewater. Particulate matter in the air can be reduced by fitting smokestacks with electrostatic precipitators, fabric filters, scrubbers and inertial separators. Smoke can be reduced by using oil or gas instead of coal in factories. Noise can be reduced by using machinery that generates less noise, fitting silencers and using earplugs and earphones.
